1. Development of the Urinary System
A 2-day-old male infant born at 38 weeks gestation is evaluated in the neonatal intensive care unit for respiratory distress. Physical examination reveals a flattened nose, recessed chin, and skin folds covering the inner corners of the eyes. The infant has not voided since birth. Bedside ultrasound reveals absent kidneys bilaterally. This condition most likely resulted from a failure in which of the following processes? ★
A.
Fusion of the inferior poles of the kidneys across the midline.
B.
Mutual induction between the ureteric bud and metanephric blastema.
✔ Correct
C.
Proper subdivision of the cloaca by the growing urorectal septum.
D.
Obliteration of the allantois to form the median umbilical ligament.
E.
Complete regression of the pronephric and mesonephric duct systems.
Explanation
A. Fusion of the inferior poles of the kidneys results in a horseshoe kidney. This condition typically presents with normal renal function, although it may be associated with an increased risk of urinary tract infections or stones.
B. Renal agenesis results from a failure of the ureteric bud to interact with the metanephric blastema. Without this reciprocal induction, the metanephric mesenchyme does not differentiate into nephrons and the ureteric bud does not branch. Bilateral renal agenesis leads to oligohydramnios, which causes the compressed physical features (Potter sequence) and pulmonary hypoplasia seen here.
C. Abnormal division of the cloaca by the urorectal septum leads to urorectal or vesicovaginal fistulas, not renal agenesis. This process occurs during weeks 4 to 7 to separate the urogenital sinus from the anorectal canal.
D. Failure of the allantois to obliterate results in a patent urachus, which would present as urine leaking from the umbilicus rather than absent kidneys and the Potter sequence.
E. The pronephros and mesonephros are temporary kidney systems that naturally regress. Failure of the pronephros to regress is not associated with bilateral renal agenesis; the metanephros is the structure responsible for the permanent kidney.
